title: Second-Order Linear Equations, Theory and Structure
subtitle: Superposition, linear independence, the Wronskian, Abel's identity, and the constant coefficient solution machine.
description: Superposition, linear independence, the Wronskian, Abel's identity, and the constant coefficient solution machine.

atAGlance.title: Unit 9 at a Glance: Core Sub-Topics
atAGlance.summary: A second-order linear equation reads $a(x)y'' + b(x)y' + c(x)y = g(x)$; this unit builds the homogeneous theory and the constant-coefficient solution machine.

bullets.body: two side conditions pin a unique solution; linear combinations of solutions are solutions.
$W(y_1,y_2)=y_1y_2'-y_2y_1'$ tests linear independence and certifies a fundamental set.
the Wronskian itself solves a first-order ODE, so it is either never zero or identically zero.
one known solution $y_1$ manufactures a second, independent $y_2$.
the characteristic equation $ar^2+br+c=0$ splits into real-distinct, repeated, and complex cases.
the $x^m$ trick and the degree-$n$ characteristic polynomial.

modules.title: Introduction to Second-Order Equations
modules.topics: The general linear second-order equation is $a(x)y'' + b(x)y' + c(x)y = g(x)$. It is \strong{homogeneous} when $g(x)=0$ and \strong{nonhomogeneous} otherwise; its general solution carries \emph{two} arbitrary constants.

formulas.title: Standard and Normalized Forms
formulas.body:
\[ a(x)y'' + b(x)y' + c(x)y = g(x), \qquad
   y'' + p(x)y' + q(x)y = r(x), \]
with $p=b/a$, $q=c/a$, $r=g/a$ where $a(x)\neq 0$. Linearity means $y$, $y'$, $y''$
each appear to the first power and are never multiplied together.

modules.title: Existence, Uniqueness, Superposition, and Independence
modules.topics: On any interval where $p$, $q$, and $r$ are continuous, the initial value problem with $y(x_0)=y_0$, $y'(x_0)=y_0'$ has a \emph{unique} solution.

formulas.title: Superposition Principle
formulas.body:
If $y_1$ and $y_2$ each solve the homogeneous equation
$y''+p\,y'+q\,y=0$, then for \emph{any} constants $c_1,c_2$,
\[ y = c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2 \]
is also a solution. The general solution is this two-parameter family precisely
when $y_1,y_2$ are linearly independent.

modules.title: Linear Independence and the Wronskian
modules.topics: Two solutions form a \strong{fundamental set} exactly when they are linearly independent, and the Wronskian is the determinant that tests it.

formulas.title: The Wronskian
formulas.body:
\[ W(y_1,y_2)(x) = \begin{vmatrix} y_1 & y_2 \\ y_1' & y_2' \end{vmatrix}
   = y_1 y_2' - y_2 y_1'. \]
If $W(x_0)\neq 0$ at any point, $y_1,y_2$ are \strong{linearly independent} on the
interval and $\{y_1,y_2\}$ is a fundamental set.

modules.title: Abel's Identity
modules.topics: Abel's identity says the Wronskian of two solutions of $y''+p(x)y'+q(x)y=0$ itself satisfies a first-order ODE, so its sign can never change.

methodology.title: Abel's Formula
steps: For $y'' + p(x)\,y' + q(x)\,y = 0$, \[ W(x) = W(x_0)\,\exp\!\left(-\int_{x_0}^{x} p(t)\,dt\right). \] Because the exponential is never zero, $W$ is \strong{either identically zero} (dependent solutions) \strong{or never zero} (independent) --- there is no middle ground.

modules.title: Reduction of Order
modules.topics: Given one nonzero solution $y_1$ of the homogeneous equation, reduction of order substitutes $y_2 = v(x)\,y_1$ and solves for $v$, producing a second independent solution.

methodology.title: Reduction of Order Formula
steps: For $y'' + p(x)\,y' + q(x)\,y = 0$ with a known solution $y_1$, \[ y_2 = y_1(x)\int \frac{e^{-\int p(x)\,dx}}{y_1(x)^2}\,dx. \] The substitution $y_2=v y_1$ collapses the second-order equation to a \emph{first-order} equation in $v'$, which the integral above solves directly.

modules.title: Constant Coefficient Homogeneous Equations
modules.topics: For $ay'' + by' + cy = 0$ with constants $a,b,c$, try $y=e^{rx}$ to get the \strong{characteristic equation} $ar^2 + br + c = 0$. The discriminant $b^2-4ac$ selects one of three cases.

formulas.title: The Three Root Cases
formulas.body:
\[ ar^2 + br + c = 0, \qquad r = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}. \]
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=3pt]
    \item \strong{Real distinct} ($b^2-4ac>0$), roots $r_1\neq r_2$:
          \[ y = c_1 e^{r_1 x} + c_2 e^{r_2 x}. \]
    \item \strong{Repeated} ($b^2-4ac=0$), one root $r$:
          \[ y = (c_1 + c_2 x)\,e^{r x}. \]
    \item \strong{Complex conjugate} ($b^2-4ac<0$), $r=\alpha\pm\beta i$:
          \[ y = e^{\alpha x}\big(c_1\cos\beta x + c_2\sin\beta x\big). \]
\end{itemize}

warning.title: Common Pitfall: The Repeated-Root Boundary
warning.body:
The repeated case ($b^2-4ac=0$) is the boundary between real and complex roots.
Do \emph{not} write $y=(c_1+c_2)e^{rx}$ --- that is a single constant; the second
solution carries the factor $x$, giving $y=(c_1+c_2 x)e^{rx}$. For complex roots,
$\alpha$ is the \emph{real} part (in the exponential) and $\beta$ the
\emph{imaginary} part (inside the sine and cosine); swapping them is the classic
sign trap.

modules.title: The Cauchy--Euler Equation
modules.topics: The Cauchy--Euler (equidimensional) equation $ax^2y'' + bxy' + cy = 0$ has powers of $x$ matching derivative orders. Try $y=x^m$.

formulas.title: Cauchy--Euler Root Cases
formulas.body:
Substituting $y=x^m$ gives the indicial equation $am(m-1)+bm+c=0$:
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=3pt]
    \item \strong{Distinct} $m_1\neq m_2$: \quad $y = c_1 x^{m_1} + c_2 x^{m_2}$.
    \item \strong{Repeated} $m$: \quad $y = (c_1 + c_2\ln x)\,x^{m}$.
    \item \strong{Complex} $m=\alpha\pm\beta i$:
          \[ y = x^{\alpha}\big(c_1\cos(\beta\ln x) + c_2\sin(\beta\ln x)\big). \]
\end{itemize}

modules.title: Higher-Order Linear Equations
modules.topics: An $n$th-order constant-coefficient equation has a degree-$n$ characteristic polynomial; each real root contributes an exponential, and a root of \strong{multiplicity} $k$ contributes $k$ independent terms.

formulas.title: Repeated Roots at Higher Order
formulas.body:
A root $r$ of multiplicity $k$ contributes
\[ e^{r x},\; x e^{r x},\; x^2 e^{r x},\; \ldots,\; x^{k-1} e^{r x}, \]
and a complex pair $\alpha\pm\beta i$ of multiplicity $k$ contributes the analogous
$x^j e^{\alpha x}\cos\beta x$ and $x^j e^{\alpha x}\sin\beta x$ for $j=0,\ldots,k-1$.

concept.title: Unit 9 Key Takeaway
concept.body:
The general solution of a homogeneous second-order linear equation is
$\highlight{c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2}$ for any
\highlight{linearly independent} pair, which the
\highlight{Wronskian} certifies and \highlight{Abel's
identity} guards. For constant coefficients the
\highlight{characteristic roots} dictate the form ---
\highlight{real-distinct}, \highlight{repeated}
(carry the $x$), or \highlight{complex} ($e^{\alpha x}$ times sines
and cosines) --- and \highlight{reduction of order} recovers a second
solution from a first.

cheat.subtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Second-Order Linear Equations, Theory and Structure

takeaway.title: Unit 9 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
Every constant-coefficient solution is read straight off the
\highlight{characteristic roots}: real-distinct roots give
\highlight{$c_1 e^{r_1 x} + c_2 e^{r_2 x}$}, a repeated root carries
the factor $x$ as \highlight{$(c_1 + c_2 x)e^{rx}$}, and a complex
pair gives \highlight{$e^{\alpha x}(c_1\cos\beta x + c_2\sin\beta x)$}.
The \highlight{Wronskian} confirms independence,
\highlight{Abel's identity} predicts it, and
\highlight{reduction of order} recovers the missing partner ---
exactly the $x^2\ln x$ that the repeated Cauchy--Euler root demands.
